board.verdict: The through-line advances structurally while the adversary marshals a statistical counter: the BIS 2025 Triennial Survey puts the dollar on one side of 89.2 percent of all FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022. The desk reads that number against the grain. FX transactions are dominated by speculative flows and interbank repo — inertia instruments, not choices renewed. The indicators that track structural shift read differently: dollar reserve share at 58 percent from 72 percent in 2001; China settling roughly half its foreign trade in yuan; mBridge at $55 billion cumulative, 95 percent in digital yuan. The Hormuz yuan toll ended with the Iran deal. The architecture it was a symptom of did not.
board.watching: BRICS Bridge from agenda to adopted framework at the India summit; China May NBS data (June 15); whether BRICS Pay technical coordination advances at the September summit.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions, up from 88.4 percent in 2022 — is the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Currency transaction share is dominated by speculative and hedging flows, not by the reserve accumulation and trade-invoicing trends that determine long-run hegemony. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER.

board.verdict: Xi crossed the Yalu and 'denuclearization' did not survive the trip. The Tumen road bridge opens June 19 — five days from the desk's writing. No delays reported. Pyongyang will have road access to Russia that bypasses Beijing's customs infrastructure. The desk is watching cargo type and volumes after opening, not the ceremony. Kim's June 9 naval expansion announcement — a second guided missile destroyer, a stated 10,000-ton warship, explicit framing of sea-based nuclear deterrent — represents the next stage: from deterrence in place to projection capability. This was not in prior briefings and is now on the watch list.
board.watching: Cargo volumes and types on the Tumen bridge after June 19; Beijing customs response at the 1959 rail bridge; whether naval expansion (destroyer class, 10,000-ton) moves to keel-laying.

sources.label: IP Defense Forum — road bridge leverage

board.verdict: The architecture advances — unified force at 15,000, gold custody, franc-zone exit on schedule — but April's coordinated assault on Gao, Menaka, and Kidal showed the insurgency's reach, and the Russian guarantee failed in public view. Traoré's acquisition of Boungou and Wahgnion mines (SOPAMIB portfolio, announced early June) brings six of fifteen industrial mines under majority Burkinabè ownership. Sovereignty first, then capacity. The second part is now urgent.
board.watching: Gold revenue allocated to the unified force post-Kidal; renegotiated security arrangements; Faso Mêbo infrastructure delivery timelines.

board.verdict: China's May NBS data — industrial output, retail sales, and fixed-asset investment — releases imminently (expected June 15). The desk's prediction (FAI exits contraction by Q4) depends on whether the sequential trend from April's -1.6% cumulative is reversing. The Caixin May manufacturing PMI (51.8) marks six straight months above 50, suggesting the productive-capacity side of the economy is holding. Retail sales at +0.2% YoY in April remain the weak signal; if May confirms the trend, the demand-side gap widens going into the July Politburo. The semiconductor bet continues to pay: exports +66.5% in May, record trade surplus.
board.watching: May NBS data (June 15): FAI, retail sales, industrial output; whether July Politburo signals demand-side intervention; semiconductor export growth compression as tariff response builds.

board.verdict: The United States entered a direct war against Iran and is now exiting on terms that include $24 billion in frozen asset releases, no nuclear dismantlement, and Iran's FM on record that 'our sword will always hang over the Strait of Hormuz.' The deal text is agreed as of June 13; formal signing is pending — the nuclear chapter (US claims dismantlement; Iran insists on enrichment rights) remains the unresolved spine. CENTCOM shot down Iranian drones near Hormuz on the same day the deal was announced. The capacity to initiate and the will to sustain are not the same thing; the United States demonstrated the first and failed the second.
board.watching: Whether the nuclear chapter resolves or collapses the deal before June 30; formal signing ceremony; pledge-to-delivery ratio on military aid to Ukraine.

wars.basis: The desk holds the Russia-decisive call while acknowledging what the material record for January through May 2026 requires. Russian territorial advances across those five months ran at approximately 40 square kilometers, against 516 square kilometers in the same period of 2025 — a ninety-percent decline in the pace of advance. The desk does not cite ISW as authoritative; it notes that independent Western counters with different methodologies converge on the order of magnitude of slowdown, and convergence at that scale is itself a material fact. What this does not mean: Russia is not losing. Russia fights at the pace its industry sustains; sustaining attrition and drone tempo rather than territorial acquisition is a coherent military posture calibrated to exhausting Ukrainian political will over time. The drone tempo is unchanged: 118 drones overnight on June 13. Martyanov's June 12 post — 'You Cannot Be Ready' — is the desk's clearest signal: he is writing about German military unreadiness for Russian action by 2029, not about Ukrainian collapse in the coming months. The desk's standing reference has already extended his own horizon past the current front. The desk revises the timeline expectation: Russia-decisive means the war ends on Russia's terms — not necessarily on Russia's original schedule. This is going longer. Russian missile production is running 'just in time' with low stockpile buffers; the Ukrainian deep-strike campaign targeting supply-chain nodes (Cheboksary navigation-module plant, June 10) introduces a new question about sustainment. The desk watches this without moving the call.

wars.advantage: Contested — deal text agreed, nuclear chapter unresolved, awaiting signing
wars.basis: US and Israel initiated strikes on Iran on February 28, 2026. Pakistan's PM Sharif declared on June 13 that 'a final, agreed upon text' of a peace deal has been reached; a senior US official confirmed signing expected 'in the coming days.' Reported terms: 60-day ceasefire extension; Hormuz reopened without tolls within 30 days; 15-20 year nuclear freeze with enrichment site dismantlement; $24 billion in frozen Iranian assets released. Against that: Iran's FM Araghchi said on the same day that Tehran had 'not reached a final conclusion' and that 'our sword will always hang over the Strait of Hormuz'; IRNA reported Iran insisting on enrichment rights. CENTCOM shot down multiple Iranian drones near Hormuz on June 13. No new developments June 14. The gap between what the US official described (nuclear dismantlement) and what Iran's state media described (enrichment rights retained) is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. The desk filed a prediction June 14 that formal signing occurs before June 30.

AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

items.text: The front has hardened: Russia-decisive call holds with timeline revision; Jan-May 2026 advance pace 92% below 2025; Martyanov extends horizon to 2029; attrition tempo maintained (118 drones June 13)
DPRK: Tumen bridge June 19 countdown — no delays reported; Kim naval expansion (destroyer, 10,000-ton warship) now on watch list
The BIS number and what it does not settle: dollar FX share 89.2% is the adversary's argument; reserve share and trade settlement tell the structural story

watching: Tumen bridge cargo volumes and Beijing customs response (June 19 opening)
China May NBS data: FAI, retail sales, industrial output (releases June 15)
Iran deal formal signing (text agreed June 13; nuclear chapter unresolved)
Russian production sustainment vs. Ukrainian supply-chain deep-strike pressure

items.text: The Islamabad MOU: Iran deal text agreed, Hormuz drones fly anyway, nuclear chapter deferred
Russia Day on the refineries: Tatarstan and Samara struck, 'brotherly people' language dropped from Russian discourse
Beijing reads the oil signal: Iran deal drops price 3%, wartime stockpiling unwinds, yuan toll ends — structure holds

watching: US-Iran nuclear chapter gap (enrichment vs. freeze — the deal's unresolved spine)
Tumen bridge traffic after June 19 opening
Samara-Tatarstan refinery campaign impact on Russian export revenue
China June FAI and manufacturing data (before July Politburo)

items.text: Trump names the island: Kharg seizure threat, ceasefire 'meaningless,' negotiations elevated
The Tumen road: Russia-DPRK first road bridge opens June 19, second land corridor for Pyongyang
Ukraine — the pace that wins: Zubr serial production, NATO doctrine gap acknowledged, Geneva talks frozen

watching: Iran ceasefire extension or collapse (before June 25)
DPRK-Russia bridge traffic and Beijing customs response
Samara CDU restart timeline
China June manufacturing/FAI data
AES gold-revenue allocation to unified force

items.text: Two blows to the logistics chain: Davydov car bomb, Kuibyshev refinery offline
Kharg Island, spared for now: US strikes military targets, threatens oil infrastructure
Beijing's May numbers: exports +19.4%, semiconductors +66.5%, narrative loses again
The standing reference reads the air defense ledger: Hodges confirms 9/9 Kyiv unintercepted

watching: Iran war ceasefire and Kharg Island oil infrastructure
China June trade and FAI data
Samara CDU restart timeline
DPRK nuclear-state language at multilateral forums
AES gold-revenue allocation to unified force

items.text: Xi crosses the Yalu, again — and declines to mention the bomb
Kidal falls, and the AES counts its dead (April events — filed stale; see Corrections)

watching: DPRK nuclear recognition at multilateral forums
AES gold-revenue allocation post-Kidal
BRICS Bridge at the India summit
China manufacturing investment, June data
Pledge-to-delivery ratio on Western aid

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.
